title: Mini Fridge Snack Cabinet
summary: A cabinet sized to house a mini fridge with a drawer above and a shelf that can hold snacks or come out for a taller fridge. Legs, drawer, and a real top.
description:
A small cabinet built around a mini fridge: an open bay that fits a compact fridge, a fixed lower shelf, an optional middle shelf for snacks (leave it out and the bay swallows a taller fridge), a drawer up top, and a finished slab top. It's the piece for a dorm, bonus room, or office nook — hides the fridge and gives you drawer and shelf storage in the same footprint.

The carcass is plywood ripped to 15-3/4" strips (called '1x16' in the plan) on 2x2 legs, trimmed to look built. Go formaldehyde-free on the sheet good if you can — it lives in a room where people eat. The bottom shelf sits 3/4" up from the bottom of the sides and is fixed; the middle shelf can ride on shelf pins so you can adjust or remove it for fridge clearance.

The drawer is the one part that demands precision. Build it to the opening and the specific 16" euro slides, install it in its highest position first, and confirm it slides sweetly before committing. It sits inset 3/4" so the applied face lands flush — attach that face last with an even gap all around.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Cut into 15-3/4" strips (the '1x16' in this plan). Formaldehyde-free preferred.

materials.name: Shelf pins
materials.note: For the optional adjustable middle shelf.

cutList.note: One is the optional middle/adjustable shelf.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two sides at 28", two shelves at 24" and one top panel at 29" from the 3/4" plywood. Cut four side trim at 15-3/4", two drawer trim at 24" and one top trim (1x2) at 29" from the 1x2. Cut four legs at 30" and three front/back trim at 24" from the 2x2. Cut one top trim (1x3) at 29", two drawer front/back at 21-1/2" and two drawer sides at 16" from the 1x3. Cut one drawer bottom at 23" from the 1/4" plywood. Cut one drawer face at 23-3/4" from the 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 8 ft 1x2, three 8 ft 2x2 and two 8 ft 1x3. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Trim the sides
steps.body: Glue and nail the four 15-3/4" pieces of 1x2 side trim to the plywood sides with 1-1/4" finish nails — one across the top edge and one across the bottom edge of each side, hiding the raw plywood ply lines that would otherwise show on a piece of furniture. Each trim piece is 15-3/4", exactly the sides' 15-3/4" width, so hold it flush at both ends and the front and back edges take care of themselves. Wipe the squeeze-out before it skins over: dried glue under paint or stain shows up as a pale patch you cannot sand out later without going through the veneer.

steps.title: Add the legs
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the front and back edges of each plywood side, then fasten a 30" 2x2 leg to each edge with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, so the legs stand vertically at the four corners of the finished cabinet. The legs are 30" and the sides only 28", so the side does not cover the full length of the leg — set the same offset at the bottom of both sides, or the cabinet will sit crooked no matter how square the box is. Clamp each leg to its side before driving screws; a 2x2 will creep along the joint as pocket screws pull tight. Check each side assembly still lies flat on the bench when you are done, because a leg pulled out of plane here becomes a drawer opening that isn't square later.

steps.title: Install the shelves
steps.body: Stand the two side assemblies up and fit the two 24" shelves between them; with a 3/4" plywood side at each end that makes the carcass 25-1/2" wide overall. Fix the lower shelf permanently — it sits 3/4" up from the bottom of the sides — with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, drilled on the underside where they will never be seen. The second shelf is the snack shelf and is where the flexibility lives: either pocket-screw it in place, or drill a matching row of shelf-pin holes in each side and rest it on four shelf pins so it can move up, down, or come out entirely when a taller fridge goes in. Check the box square across the diagonals before anything sets, because the drawer opening above is measured off it.

steps.title: Trim the bottom shelf and add the top support
steps.body: Face the bottom shelf's front edge with one of the three 24" 2x2 front/back trim pieces, then fit the other two across the top of the case, one at the front and one at the back, level with each other. Those two are what the drawer rides against — they carry the slide cleats and stiffen the top of the opening, so if they are not level with each other the drawer will never sit square in its hole. Fasten all three with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws driven into the sides. Measure the finished opening between the two sides now and write the number down: the drawer box is built to fit the opening, not the other way round.

steps.title: Build and attach the top
steps.body: Glue and nail the two 29" top trim pieces to the 29" x 15-3/4" plywood top panel — the 2-1/2"-wide 1x3 along one long edge and the 1-1/2"-wide 1x2 along the other — then sand the joints flush so the finished top reads as one slab rather than a panel with strips stuck on it. Set the top on the cabinet and centre it, making the overhang even at both ends; at 29" it is deliberately longer than the carcass. Fasten it by driving 2" finish nails up through the 2x2 legs and the top front/back supports into the underside of the top, which leaves no fastener showing on the surface people will actually set drinks on.

steps.title: Build the drawer
steps.body: Build the drawer box 23" wide by 16" deep. The two 16" 1x3 drawer sides run the full 16" depth, and the two 21-1/2" front and back pieces fit between them — 21-1/2" plus 3/4" of stock at each end is the 23" outside width, and that matches the 23" x 16" 1/4" plywood bottom exactly, so use the bottom as your squaring gauge. Join the corners with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, then glue and brad the plywood bottom onto the bottom edges and check the diagonals match before the glue grabs. The 23" is not arbitrary: the opening between the cabinet sides is 24", and the missing inch is the 1/2" of clearance a euro bottom-mount slide needs on each side. A drawer box that is out of square will bind on those slides no matter how carefully the slides themselves are mounted.

steps.title: Install the drawer and face
steps.body: Install the slides in the cabinet with the drawer in its highest possible position. Fit the drawer and confirm it slides nicely — it needs to sit inset 3/4" for the face to inset. Once happy, attach the face with an even gap all around using 1-1/4" finish nails and glue. Add the bottom trim last, sand, and finish.

images.alt: Cabinet housing a mini fridge with a drawer above and a finished top
